**Curriculum context:** This is Module 48 of the A1 track (Ukrainian for English speakers). Title: "Body & Health" — At the Doctor's. Phase: A1.5 [Modals, Commands & Life]. Previous module: Imperative And Requests. Next module: My Family.

# Module Build: Content + Activities + Vocabulary

## 1. Goal

> **You are Patient & Supportive Ukrainian Tutor, writing in the voice of Patient Supportive Tutor.**
>
> Build a complete beginner module for English-speaking teens and adults learning Ukrainian at the a1 level. Your job: **search Ukrainian school textbooks using RAG tools**, then **adapt** the pedagogy for L2 learners.
>
> **Output capacity: 65,000+ tokens.** Do NOT truncate.

**What L2 learners need** (that L1 textbooks assume):
1. Explicit grammar rules in English (L1 learners know intuitively)
2. Level-appropriate vocabulary only
3. Setting/purpose for dialogues (L1 assumes shared cultural context)

## 2. Scoring Dimensions

Your content will be scored on these 7 dimensions (see GEMINI.md for details):
1. **Experience Quality** — would the learner continue?
2. **Language Accuracy** — correct Ukrainian, no Russianisms
3. **Pedagogy** — clear progression, quick wins
4. **Activities** — variety, appropriate difficulty
5. **Beginner Safety** — warm tone, not overwhelming
6. **LLM Fingerprint** — natural voice, not robotic
7. **Linguistic Accuracy** — factual correctness

---

### Vocabulary



**Target vocabulary** (from the plan — teach and use these). Include ALL required words. Include recommended words by using them naturally in your content — they count toward your 20 vocabulary target:

### Vocabulary from Plan (MANDATORY — include ALL required items)

**Required** (MUST appear in vocabulary YAML):
- голова (head) — High frequency (Top 100); collocations: «болить голова», «головний біль», «на голові»
- рука (arm/hand) — High frequency; collocations: «права/ліва рука», «мити руки», «тримати в руках»
- нога (leg/foot) — High frequency; collocations: «права/ліва нога», «іти пішки» (on foot)
- живіт (stomach) — Med frequency; usage: «болить живіт», «спати на животі»
- горло (throat) — collocations: «червоне горло», «полоскати горло»
- болить (it hurts) — usage: 3rd person singular; agrees with body part (Subject); plural form: «болять»
- лікар (doctor) — collocations: «сімейний лікар», «записатися до лікаря»; contrast with «лікарня» (hospital)
- аптека (pharmacy) — context: «в аптеці»; note cultural sign: green cross

**Recommended** (use in your content to reach the vocabulary target):
- температура (fever/temperature) — collocations: «міряти температуру», «висока температура», «у мене температура»
- кашель (cough) — collocations: «сильний кашель», «ліки від кашлю»
- ліки (medicine) — usage: «пити ліки», «купувати ліки від...»; plural only in this context
- хворий (sick/ill) — usage: «він хворий» (state) vs «він часто хворіє» (process/frequency)
- здоровий (healthy) — proverb: «У здоровому тілі — здоровий дух»
- рецепт (prescription) — usage: «ліки за рецептом» vs «без рецепту»
- малина (raspberry) — cultural context: «чай з малиною» as a standard home remedy for colds

These are your TARGET words — teach them all and use them heavily. For the rest of the text, use natural, level-appropriate Ukrainian.

## Language Quality Rules (Beginner Tier)

### Russian Characters (HARD FAIL)

**ы, э, ё, ъ** must NEVER appear in Ukrainian text. These are Russian-only characters.

### Stress Mark Typography

Use lowercase letters with a combining acute accent (´) on the stressed vowel:
- Correct: ма́ма, анана́с, оса́, сосна́
- Wrong: мА́ма, ананА́с, осА́, соснА́ (do NOT capitalize the stressed vowel)

### IPA and Latin Transliteration (BANNED at ALL levels)

Never include IPA symbols (ɑ, ɛ, ʃ, etc.) or bracketed pronunciation guides like `[ma-ma]`, `[a-na-nas]`, `[ˈmɑmɑ]`. The ONLY pronunciation aid is the stress mark (´) on the vowel.

Latin transliterations are BANNED: never use kh, sh, ch, zh, ts, ya, yu, ye, shch.

```markdown
❌ WRONG: "мама [ˈmɑmɑ]" or "хліб (khlib)"
✅ RIGHT: "**ма́ма** (mom)" or "**Х**, like the «ch» in Scottish «loch»"
```

- **ALWAYS** use Ukrainian angular quotes: «...» (never straight quotes "...")
- Base content vocabulary on the plan's `vocabulary_hints`. Function words (pronouns, conjunctions, particles, question words) are always allowed

### No Word Salad (HARD FAIL)

# Дослідження: Body & Health

## State Standard Reference
§3 (A1 Thematic Catalogue): "здоров'я" — one of the 12 listed A1 thematic areas (lines 479–556).
§4.2.3.1: Indicative mood — present/past/future (pain expressions: **болить/болять**).
§4.3.1: Simple sentences — SVO, questions (що болить? де болить?).
§4.3.2: Basic complex sentences — and specifically **бо** (Я не прийшов, **бо** я хворий). Note: plan explicitly cites §4.3.2 and exempts this module from the general A1 subordinate clause restriction.
§4.2.1.4: Personal pronoun basic declension — **мені** (dative form) is introduced here as a formulaic chunk («Мені погано/добре»), not as full dative paradigm teaching.

**Alignment**: Core health/medical topic at A1. Pain expressions + symptom vocabulary + doctor/pharmacy functional dialogue directly address the "здоров'я" thematic area.

---

## Vocabulary Frequency

| Word | Notes | Key Collocations |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| голова | Top 100; highly productive | болить голова, головний біль, на голові |
| рука | Top 100; covers arm AND hand — flag for learners | права/ліва рука, мити руки, тримати в руках |
| нога | Top 100; covers leg AND foot — flag for learners | права/ліва нога, іти пішки |
| живіт | Mid-frequency; body-specific | болить живіт, спати на животі |
| горло | Mid-frequency; medical context | червоне горло, полоскати горло |
| болить | 3rd sg. verb; subject = body part | болить голова / болять очі |
| лікар | High frequency; false cognate trap with лікарня | сімейний лікар, записатися до лікаря |
| аптека | High frequency; practical survival vocabulary | в аптеці, зелений хрест |
| температура | Mid-frequency; covers both "fever" and "temperature" | міряти температуру, висока температура |
| хворий | Adjective + state; contrast with хворіти (process) | він хворий / він часто хворіє |
| ліки | Plural-only noun in medical sense | пити ліки, ліки від кашлю, за рецептом |
| малина | Low frequency; culturally loaded folk-remedy | чай з малиною |

---

## Cultural Hooks

1. **Proverb (школа/спорт)**: «У здоровому тілі — здоровий дух» — used in Ukrainian schools and sports contexts. Attributed to Roman satirist Juvenal but deeply embedded in Ukrainian school culture. Learnable as a whole chunk; introduces **здоровий** in a memorable frame.

2. **Folk remedy tradition (траволікування)**: Ukrainian home medicine for cold/flu — чай з малиною (raspberry tea), чай з калиною (viburnum), меду ложка. Pharmacies stock folk-remedy teas (трав'яні чаї). Real-world connection: when a Ukrainian says «Попий чаю з малиною», this IS the first-line treatment for a cold.

3. **Green cross sign (аптека)**: The green cross (зелений хрест) universally marks pharmacies in Ukraine. Distinction between «Аптека» (full pharmacy) and «Аптечний пункт» (dispensary kiosk). Learners visiting Ukraine can immediately apply this visual knowledge — high practical value.

---

## Common Learner Errors

1. **«Я болю»** → **«Мені болить»** — English "I hurt/I am in pain" maps onto 1st person, but Ukrainian constructs pain as impersonal: the body part hurts. The body part is the grammatical subject; the person is experiencer in dative/genitive construction.

2. **Agreement mismatch: «У мене болять голова»** → **«У мене болить голова»** — English "my eyes hurt" = plural verb, which learners over-apply. Rule: verb agrees with the body-part subject. **болить** (sg.) / **болять** (pl. — «болять очі, болять ноги»).

3. **«Я йду в лікар»** → **«Я йду до лікаря»** — Confusing the person **лікар** with the place **лікарня**. "Going to the doctor" = direction toward a person → genitive + **до**. "Going to hospital" = **до лікарні**. This error is in the plan's teaching points — prioritize.

4. **«Я погано»** → **«Мені погано»** — English "I feel bad" → direct translation produces non-sentence in Ukrainian. «Мені погано» is impersonal; «я» is not the subject. Teach as a fixed chunk before explaining the structure.

---

## Cross-References

- **Builds on**: a1-30 (Prepositions: Direction & Origin) — locative «на голові», «в аптеці»; genitive «до лікаря»
- **Builds on**: earlier modules covering present tense conjugation (M15+), past tense (M36)
- **Prepares for**: a1-49 (My Family) — describing family members' health; using болить for others
- **Prepares for**: a1-62 (Emergencies) — escalated health situations, calling for help

---

## Teaching Strategy

- **Discovery exercise**: Show 3 sentences in English: "My head hurts." / "My legs hurt." / "I feel bad." Then show the Ukrainian: «Болить голова» / «Болять ноги» / «Мені погано». Ask: What is different about how Ukrainian expresses this compared to English? Who is the "main actor" in each sentence? Let the learner notice that the BODY PART is the subject, not the person.

- **Best context**: Doctor's waiting room or a phone call to a friend explaining why you can't come — the plan's dialogue structure (formal polyclinic vs. casual friend) provides both registers naturally.

- **Contrastive pairs for disambiguation**:
  - **лікар** (person) vs **лікарня** (place) — «до лікаря» vs «до лікарні»
  - **болить** (singular subject) vs **болять** (plural subject) — «болить голова» vs «болять ноги»
  - **хворий** (state adjective: він хворий today) vs **хворіти** (process verb: він часто хворіє)
  - **за рецептом** vs **без рецепту** — pharmacy interaction pair

---

## Notes for Content Writing

- **«Рука» and «нога» ambiguity**: Flag explicitly that рука = arm AND hand, нога = leg AND foot. English learners expect separate words. A quick visual table (рука → ✋ arm + hand) prevents confusion.
- **Plural irregulars**: Plan mentions **око/очі, вухо/вуха** — introduce as visual pairs with a note "Ukrainian has some special plural forms for paired body parts." Don't over-explain; show the pattern.
- **«Мені» chunk teaching**: Introduce «Мені погано» / «Мені добре» as frozen expressions BEFORE explaining they involve dative. Say: "In Ukrainian, you say 'to me it is bad' — use this phrase as a whole."
- **Imperative forms available** (M47 introduced them): «Дайте, будь ласка...», «Скажіть, де аптека?», «Відкрийте рот» — use imperatives freely in dialogue sections.
- **Register contrast**: The plan calls for two dialogues — casual (friend) vs. formal (полікліника). Keep Ukrainian sentences ≤10 words per A1 sentence constraint.


## Resource Discovery

### Videos
- [FMU 1-53 | How to describe what hurts in Ukrainian | 5 Minute Ukrainian](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DdZjJ4H24a4) (Ukrainian Lessons) — Directly covers describing pain, body parts, and a dialogue about visiting a doctor, which are core topics of this module.
- [ULP 5-170 | 15 фразеологізмів з частинами тіла | Ukrainian Lessons Podcast Season 5](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pD8eA7W5ZQw) (Ukrainian Lessons) — Covers phraseologisms (idioms) related to body parts, which tangentially relates to 'народна мудрість' (folk wisdom) and 'Частини тіла' (body parts), but doesn't directly address describing pain or health scenarios.

- [Ukrainian Phrasebook: Health](https://www.ukrainianlessons.com/ph-health/) (ukrainianlessons.com)

messages.content:
===CONTENT_START===
## Вступ: Частини тіла та народна мудрість (Introduction: Body Parts & Folk Wisdom)

Welcome back! So far, we have talked about our daily routines, going to cafes, and asking for directions. Today, we are going to dive into a very important topic: health and the human body. Knowing how to talk about your body is a vital survival skill in any language, whether you are buying simple medicine or visiting a clinic. 

First, let us learn some of the most high-frequency body parts in Ukrainian. The word for head is **голова́**. We also have **рука́** and **нога́**. There is an important difference here between Ukrainian and English that you should flag for yourself right now. In English, you have different words for "arm" and "hand". In Ukrainian, **рука́** covers both the arm and the hand! Similarly, **нога́** covers both the leg and the foot. It is all one continuous concept in everyday speech. If you need to specify the exact part, there are medical terms, but for daily use, **рука́** and **нога́** are all you need. We also have **живі́т**, which means stomach or belly.

Let us look at some important visual pairs for body parts. Ukrainian has some special plural forms for paired body parts that you simply need to memorize. 
*   **о́ко** (eye) becomes **о́чі** (eyes)
*   **ву́хо** (ear) becomes **ву́ха** (ears)

Do you remember our prepositions from module thirty? You already know how to talk about location using the question word **де** (where). You can easily combine these body parts with the preposition **на**.
*   **на голові́** — on the head
*   **на рука́х** — on the hands (or arms)
*   **на нога́х** — on the feet (or legs)

In Ukrainian school and sports culture, there is a very famous proverb that children learn early on. It perfectly captures the concept of being healthy, which in Ukrainian is **здоро́вий** (healthy). The proverb is:
*   «**У здоро́вому ті́лі — здоро́вий дух**» (In a healthy body, a healthy spirit).

This phrase introduces the word **здоро́вий** in a memorable frame. Whenever Ukrainians talk about exercise or physical education, you will likely hear this proverb. It is a fantastic cultural hook that connects language learning to a positive lifestyle!

## Презентація: Як ми говоримо про біль (Presentation: Expressing Pain & Symptoms)

Now that we know the basic body parts, how do we say that something hurts? This is where Ukrainian grammar gets very interesting and differs significantly from English. In English, you might say "I have a headache" or "My leg hurts." In Ukrainian, the core grammar structure is «**У ме́не боли́ть** + [Subject]». 

Literally translated, this means "At me hurts the [body part]." In this sentence, the body part is the grammatical subject. You are the experiencer, not the actor. Let us look at the verb **боли́ть** (it hurts). Because the body part is the subject, the verb must agree with it. If one thing hurts, we use the singular **боли́ть**. If multiple things hurt, we use the plural form **боля́ть**. 

Let us practice this with some examples to make the pattern clear:
*   «**У ме́не боли́ть голова́.**» — I have a headache. (Literally: At me hurts the head.)
*   «**У ме́не боли́ть живі́т.**» — My stomach hurts. 
*   «**У ме́не боля́ть о́чі.**» — My eyes hurt. (Notice the plural **боля́ть** matching **о́чі**.)
*   «**У ме́не боля́ть но́ги.**» — My legs hurt.

A very common learner error is to mismatch the agreement. You might be tempted to say ~~«У ме́не боля́ть голова́»~~, thinking about the English phrase "My head hurts," but this is incorrect. Always remember: singular body part means **боли́ть**, plural body parts mean **боля́ть**. Contrast the incorrect ~~«У ме́не боля́ть голова́»~~ with the correct «**У ме́не боли́ть голова́**». The body part dictates the verb.

Beyond specific pain, how do you express your general state of feeling? The State Standard requires us to learn impersonal phrases for state. If you want to say "I feel bad," you must use the dative pronoun **мені́** with an adverb.
*   «**Мені́ пога́но.**» — I feel bad. 
*   «**Мені́ до́бре.**» — I feel good.

Drill this carefully to avoid the direct translation error of saying ~~«Я пога́но»~~. In Ukrainian, «**Я пога́но**» is not a valid sentence because states of being happen *to* you. Treat «**Мені́ пога́но**» as a frozen chunk of vocabulary for now. 

Let us add some more symptom vocabulary:
*   **температу́ра** — fever or temperature (collocations: **висо́ка температу́ра**, **мі́ряти температу́ру**)
*   **ка́шель** — cough (collocations: **си́льний ка́шель**)
*   **не́жить** — runny nose
*   **головни́й біль** — headache 

When you have these symptoms, Ukrainians often turn to home remedies before visiting a clinic. The Ukrainian tradition of **траволікува́ння** (phytotherapy or herbal medicine) is very strong. If you tell a Ukrainian friend «**Мені́ пога́но**», they will immediately suggest drinking tea! The most popular remedies include tea with **мали́на** (raspberry), **кали́на** (viburnum berries), or a large spoonful of **мед** (honey). «**Чай з мали́ною**» is practically the official national cure for a common cold.

## Діалог: У лікаря та в аптеці (Dialogue: At the Doctor's & Pharmacy)

When raspberry tea is not enough, it is time to visit a professional. Your first point of contact is usually the **сіме́йний лі́кар** (family doctor). Here, we must address another very common learner error. The word for the person is **лі́кар** (doctor), but the word for the building is **ліка́рня** (hospital). 

If you are going to the doctor, you are moving toward a person, so you use the genitive case with the preposition **до**.
*   Correct: «**Я йду до лі́каря.**» — I am going to the doctor.
*   Correct: «**Я йду до ліка́рні.**» — I am going to the hospital.
*   Incorrect: ~~«Я йду в лі́кар.»~~ — This means you are physically walking inside the doctor, which is impossible!

After seeing the doctor, you will likely need to visit the pharmacy, which is called **апте́ка**. Navigating Ukrainian streets to find one is easy: just look for the illuminated green cross (**зеле́ний хрест**). You might see two types of signs: an **Апте́ка** is a full pharmacy, while an **Апте́чний пункт** is a smaller dispensary kiosk, often located inside a clinic.

Let us look at some functional language for the pharmacy. The word for medicine is **лі́ки**. In Ukrainian, **лі́ки** is a plural-only noun, just like "clothes" or "scissors" in English. You will use it to ask for medicine against specific symptoms.
*   «**Да́йте, будь ла́ска, лі́ки від ка́шлю.**» — Please give me medicine for a cough.
*   «**Да́йте лі́ки від головно́го бо́лю.**» — Give me medicine for a headache.

You also need to understand the difference between getting medicine **за реце́птом** (with a prescription) versus **без реце́пту** (without a prescription). 

Let us read a typical dialogue at the polyclinic, where the doctor asks «**Що вас турбу́є?**» (What bothers you?).

> — **Добри́день. Що вас турбу́є?**
> — Good afternoon. What bothers you?
> — **Добри́день. Мені́ пога́но. У ме́не висо́ка температу́ра і ка́шель.**
> — Good afternoon. I feel bad. I have a high fever and a cough.
> — **У вас боли́ть го́рло?**
> — Does your throat hurt?
> — **Так, ду́же боли́ть.**
> — Yes, it hurts a lot.
> — **Ось реце́пт. Купі́ть ці лі́ки в апте́ці.**
> — Here is the prescription. Buy this medicine at the pharmacy.

## Практика: Пояснення причин (Practice: Explaining Causes)

Sometimes you need to explain to your boss, teacher, or friend why you cannot meet them. To do this, we construct basic complex sentences using the conjunctions **бо** and **тому́ що**, which both mean "because." 

According to the State Standard, this is an essential skill for this level. You can link two simple sentences together using **бо**.
*   «**Я не прийшо́в, бо я хво́рий.**» — I did not come because I am sick.
*   «**Я вдо́ма, тому́ що у ме́не температу́ра.**» — I am at home because I have a fever.

Let us discuss an important distinction between two related words: **хво́рий** and **хворі́ти**. 
*   **хво́рий** is an adjective that describes a state of being sick right now. («**Він хво́рий.**» — He is sick.)
*   **хворі́ти** is a verb that describes the ongoing process or frequency of being ill. («**Він ча́сто хворі́є.**» — He gets sick often.)

Also, we must address one more pitfall. Never say ~~«Я болю́»~~! In Ukrainian, this would mean "I cause pain." If you are the one experiencing the pain, you must use the impersonal structure we practiced earlier: «**Мені́ боли́ть**» or «**У ме́не боли́ть**». 

Let us look at a roleplay contrasting a casual dialogue with a friend about feeling sick versus a more formal explanation.

**Casual Dialogue with a Friend:**
> — **Приві́т! Ти йдеш у кіно́ сього́дні?**
> — Hi! Are you going to the cinema today?
> — **Ні, я вдо́ма, бо я хво́рий.**
> — No, I am at home because I am sick.
> — **Що ста́лося? Що боли́ть?**
> — What happened? What hurts?
> — **У ме́не ду́же боли́ть го́рло і си́льний не́жить.**
> — My throat hurts very much, and I have a bad runny nose.
> — **Пий чай з мали́ною! Оду́жуй шви́дко!**
> — Drink tea with raspberry! Get well soon!

In this casual setting, the friend suggests the traditional **чай з мали́ною** and wishes a quick recovery. The sentences are short and directly explain the cause of absence using **бо**.

Let us summarize the key points of this module. When describing symptoms, always remember that the body part is the subject of your sentence. The verb **боли́ть** must match whether that body part is singular or plural. «**У ме́не боли́ть го́рло**» (singular) versus «**У ме́не боля́ть ву́ха**» (plural). When you feel unwell overall, use the fixed dative chunk «**Мені́ пога́но**». Finally, always ask for medicine in the pharmacy **від** (from/against) your specific symptom, and know whether it is **за реце́птом** or **без реце́пту**.

## Підсумок

In this module, you learned how to navigate health situations in Ukrainian. You discovered high-frequency body parts like **голова́**, **рука́**, **нога́**, and **живі́т**, along with their visual pairs like **о́ко/о́чі** and **ву́хо/ву́ха**. We explored the cultural significance of the proverb «**У здоро́вому ті́лі — здоро́вий дух**» and the traditional remedy of tea with **мали́на**.

Crucially, you learned the unique Ukrainian grammar for expressing pain. You know that the body part is the subject in the phrase «**У ме́не боли́ть** / **боля́ть**», avoiding the common error of translating "My head hurts" directly from English. You practiced functional language for visiting the **сіме́йний лі́кар** and finding the **апте́ка** with the green cross to buy **лі́ки**. Finally, you learned to construct complex sentences with **бо** to explain absences, such as «**Я не прийшо́в, бо я хво́рий.**»
